but there was no riot or breach Of
the law. The banking interests noted


the demonstration and then called


a meeting for the Auditorium for
Friday afternoon.


1t WIS at that meeting on the
| afternoon of the Gath Of November.
| 1893. that George Turner uttered
| sentiments which have ever since
been remembered by the laboring
element of this city. Whether he


was there in his capacity as a
lawyer. selling his services" does
not appear upon the meager records
now obtainable of that memorable


meeting. At any rate, he was prom
inent all through the proceedings.


From the Chronicle of that date it
is learned that Mr.. Turner called
the meeting to order. Robert Eas-
son, now deceased, was made chair


man and Turner at once assumed


the role of floor manager. According


to the Chronicle, Mr.. Turner address,
ed the chair thus:


II see here some of the incen-
diaries Of yesterday. We can not


work to advantage if they are pres


ent. l move that all persons not in


sympathy with the meeting be re-


quested to retire from the hall"
The so-called incendiaries were a


number Of workingmen of the city.


who had called to look on while the


banking interests acted In the dele


CATION were John H. Roche. now a
lawyer, but then a barber; Louis
Thompson, ex-councilman; Hugh C.
Thompson, carpenter and Knight of


Labor; E. j. Tamblin, still car
penter and woodworker Of this city;


John p. Coffeen, a knight of Labor
and now superior court bailiff; w.


II. Davidson, Knight of Labor.
Scarcely had the motion been made
by Turner when H. c. Thompson, so
| runs the Chronicles account, arose


and said: Mr.. Chair"
The speaker was here interrupted.
| SO the Chronicle report states, by
| Mr.. Turner, who said:


That's one Of them. That's one
| of the incendiaries."


Mr.. Thompson, so those present
say, protested: II am a law abiding
citizen"


In the face of that declaration,
Mr.. Turner moved that a commit
tee of five be appointed to see that
such persons do retire from the room
| and 1 want to be chairman of that
committee" The motion was carried
The lateness of the hour made it
| impossible for any further report to


be made before the afternoon paper
went to press. Further information
is found in the report in the Spokane


Review Of the following morning,


from which the following excerpts
are taken:


III. c. Thompson-Mr Speaker"
Judge Turner-That man was one
of the chief incendiaries, and If he


had any sense of decency he would
get out of that door without more
ado"


-. j. Browne-The men are not


anarchists, but law-abiding citizens,
and as such were entitled to the
privileges Of free speech in a public
| meeting"


Judge Turner-It is easy to see


in these times Of danger, not only
personal, but to our brick blocks,
why our conservative brick block
owners should adopt policy of
good Lord, good devil to tide them
through. These men have proven
